hints.text: Bollards thin, slightly **curved**: rectangular front reflector + **two circular back reflectors** (white or orange), angled black strip, often numbered
Poletops w/ **alternating hook insulators (left-right-left)** — rare in the other Baltics

shared_with.note: Similar alternating hooks on wooden poles.
shared_with.diff: Latvian macrons (ā ē ī) vs Croatian č/ć.

hints.text: Chevrons white w/ red arrows (EE/RU/UA inverted); Baltic blue km markers — Latvia's **parallel to the road, number on one side** (LT: 45°, EE: perpendicular); warning-sign border **thick red + thin white outline**; **A-type guardrails w/ white or red reflectors** (EE: none, LT: orange)
Pedestrian signs **5 stripes** (Baltic-unique; EE/LT = 3) + pedestrian w/ **noticeably long legs**; bus-stop sign w/ **5-window bus** (EE/LT: 4)
hints.uniq_note: Within the Baltics both metas are Latvia-only; the long-leg pedestrian is Europe-wide distinctive.

hints.text: **Latvian**: macron vowels **Ā Ē Ī (Ū)** + hooked consonants **Ģ Ķ Ļ Ņ**; street = **iela** (unique; ultra-rare 'gatve/gat.' in Riga)
House numbers = **tall rectangles** in several colours (not common EE/LT)
Wood + **white brick** combos, steep corrugated metal roofs; pastel stone in newer areas
Directional signs blue/white w/ **more stylised arrowheads** than EE/LT
Gen4 **red or black car** (also LT; Estonia has neither)
South of **Jelgava**: wide open, much less forest + more fields than the rest of Latvia
Downtown = majestic **Art-Nouveau architecture** (Vienna-like) — unique in the whole Baltics
